title: At-least-once delivery implications
prompt_md: Most message queues guarantee **at-least-once** delivery, meaning the same job can occasionally be delivered more than once. What must your job consumer therefore be?
options: Synchronous, so the web request waits for the job to finish
Stateless, so it never stores anything to disk
Idempotent, so re-processing the same job produces the same end state and doesn't double-apply its effects
Single-threaded, so only one job runs at a time
explanation_md:
At-least-once means duplicates happen (e.g. a worker finishes but crashes before acknowledging, so the queue redelivers). The consumer must be **idempotent**: detect that it already handled this job — via a unique id / idempotency key or by asking the third party to dedupe — and skip the work the second time. Otherwise a redelivered 'charge $50' job becomes a $100 charge.

Stateless, single-threaded, and synchronous don't solve duplicate delivery — only idempotency does.
